kept.text:
I’m really struggling to see how the back of the envelope math on this works out…

There are generously 4 million characterized “software workers” in America. That’s pretty broad and includes a lot of people who aren’t really classical engineers don’t produce that much code. 

That comes out to nearly $1k per month of average Claude spend across every dev in America. Yes, there’s some international usage, but it can’t be that much. Yes there is some non software Cowork usage, but that doesn’t use that many tokens. Yes, some non engineers are using Claude to vibe code, but I really doubt many are spending hundreds per month on. 

Even if we assume 50% of all software workers are using Claude, that comes out to $2k spend per month per Claude user. Thats 10X more than the highest tier Max subscription. So almost all of Anthropics revenue has to be API billing 

So the only explanation is that something like 20%+ of software engineers are not only Claude users but on API billing and regularly spending thousands per month. At $5/m Opus tokens that means the average API user has to be going through something like 25 million tokens per day.

*OR* the other possibility is API revenue is heavily power law dominated. Maybe there’s just something like 100k super users who are making up the majority of the revenue. For that to work the typical super user would have to be spending on the order of $50k/month and guzzling nearly 1 billion tokens per day.

kept.text:
Commentary:

Saw a sharp post framing my Reddit position as a closed loop: Claude buys Reddit, Reddit hosts users asking Claude questions, Claude monetizes its own users. It's a clean joke. The actual mechanism runs differently. The loop is open.

Reddit licenses its conversation graph to every frontier model that wants to train on it. Anthropic, OpenAI, Google all pay separately. "Other revenue" ran $39M in Q1 at roughly 95% margins, annualizing to about $155M, and sell-side currently treats that line as a kicker. The actual call is whether the 2027 renewal cycle takes it from kicker to core driver. Each incremental $100M of AI-licensing revenue at 95% margins is roughly $3 to $5 per share at consensus multiples. If two of three big licensees renew at higher rates and a fourth signs (Meta, xAI, take your pick), the math compounds.

RDDT is roughly 5.6% of my book after Q1 print follow-through took the stock from a $135 entry to $170. My probability-weighted 12M target is $189: bull $245, base $185, bear $115. The position is sized for "AI-licensing TAM is bigger than consensus" rather than for the next print. Kill condition is logged-in DAU rolling negative for two consecutive quarters or a major licensee renewing flat or down. You're watching the experiment, not getting a tip.

I’m really struggling to see how the back of the envelope math on this works out…

There are generously 4 million characterized “software workers” in America. That’s pretty broad and includes a lot of people who aren’t really classical engineers don’t produce that much code. 

That comes out to nearly $1k per month of average Claude spend across every dev in America. Yes, there’s some international usage, but it can’t be that much. Yes there is some non software Cowork usage, but that doesn’t use that many tokens. Yes, some non engineers are using Claude to vibe code, but I really doubt many are spending hundreds per month on. 

Even if we assume 50% of all software workers are using Claude, that comes out to $2k spend per month per Claude user. Thats 10X more than the highest tier Max subscription. So almost all of Anthropics revenue has to be API billing 

So the only explanation is that something like 20%+ of software engineers are not only Claude users but on API billing and regularly spending thousands per month. At $5/m Opus tokens that means the average API user has to be going through something like 25 million tokens per day.

*OR* the other possibility is API revenue is heavily power law dominated. Maybe there’s just something like 100k super users who are making up the majority of the revenue. For that to work the typical super user would have to be spending on the order of $50k/month and guzzling nearly 1 billion tokens per day.

quoted_tweet.text:
@c_pick TBH I don't agree with your take. I don't think Athropic's desire to control the harness is about keeping resource usage under control. They could accomplish that by just enforcing limits on the actual resource usage (which they already do) -- if some third-party harness is inefficient, users of than harness hit their limits faster.

I think instead that they want to control the harness because if switching LLM providers is too easy, it makes business difficult for the providers. Say GPT 5.5 comes out and it's clearly smarter, faster, and cheaper than Opus 4.7. If everyone can switch providers with two clicks in their harness, many of them will. This would lead to wild revenue and usage swings, which makes capacity planning hard. And perfect competition drives down prices -- in this scenario Opus has to cut its prices to get some users back.

Obviously no business wants to be in that situation!

By controlling the harness, they add some stickiness. If switching LLM providers means switching harnesses, that's a barrier high enough that most people won't bother to do it on a whim. So now Opus 4.7 can weather the storm until 4.8 or whatever comes out and is back on top.

So it makes perfect sense to me as a business decision. It may be user-unfriendly, but tech companies do stuff like this all the time. It's nothing new.

Though I would say, it seems weird to me to do this *on top of* subscriptions. Subscriptions already create a lot of stickiness. If you're subscribed only to Claude, that's a pretty big barrier to trying out GPT quickly -- a bigger barrier than the harness barrier I think. So I question whether controlling the harness is really worth all the effort they are putting into it, but idk, they probably have insights that I don't on this.

Another factor here might actually be safety concerns. As we know, Anthropic leadership is deeply (excessively, IMO) worried about AI safety, and they feel that Anthropic will do a better job of addressing safety than any other company. They may feel that control of the harness is an important tool for that. I could definitely imagine Dario being terrified of OpenClaw from a safety perspective (I sort of am too).

These explanations make much more sense to me than the efficiency issue, which again seems like it could easily be managed in other ways. But of course, these explanations are much harder to just come out and say, without stirring a lot more outrage...

kept.text:
Claude Codeの導入検討してる企業さんからの問い合わせが増えているんだけど、よく話す内容をまとめてみる。

Claude Codeの使い方を学ぶのは大事だけど、それ以上にユーザー(従業員)のことを信じすぎないことが大事。セキュリティやリスク管理を従業員任せにすると事故る可能性が高くなる。

「APIキーをチャットに貼ったらダメ」と言ってても余裕でチャットに貼る。「機密情報は書くな」といくら言っても機密情報が何か分からずチャットに書く。人間だから、うっかりミスしてしまうこともあり得る。

使う人数が増えれば増えるほどにリスクは高くなって、どこかのタイミングで情報漏洩して大事故につながる、なんてのは企業として絶対に避けたいはず。

重要なことは、そういうのを理解した上で安全に使えるガードレールの仕組みを会社として用意しておくこと。

要するにAPIキーや機密情報を間違って送信しても、それがAnthropicなどのサーバーに送信される前に止めてやれば大事には至らない(全てを止めることはできないが)。

あとは各従業員の使用状況やどんなやりとりしているかを定期的にモニタリング、分析して改善していく仕組みもある方がいい。定着しないと意味はないし、危険な使い方をしている人がいれば、それは指導をする必要もある。

kept.text:
🔎Anthropic、ARR440億ドル超・推論マージン70%超 | 価値獲得はモデル側へ移る $NVDA

AIプラットフォームの収益化をめぐる空気が変わり始めた。5月1日に広がった市場コメントでは、AnthropicのARRが2026年に入り90億ドルから440億ドル超へ増え、推論インフラのマージンも38%から70%超へ上がったと整理された。これを、NVIDIA $NVDA のGPU需要が弱る話として受け止めると取り違える。むしろ、GPUの上で動くモデル側にも価値が残り始めたというアップデートだ。

2023年以降のAI相場は、価値の回収先がかなり分かりやすかった。訓練も推論も計算資源が足りず、ボトルネックを握ったNVIDIAに資金が集まった。AIモデル企業は成長していても、推論コストが重く、売上の伸びがそのまま利益に変わるとは見られていなかった。

今回の数字が変えているのは、その見方の硬さだ。AnthropicのARRが短期間に4倍超へ伸びるだけなら、需要の強さで説明できる。だが同じ期間に推論マージンが70%超へ改善したなら、トークンを売る事業の単価、稼働率、ソフトウェア最適化が同時に働いている。利用者が実務の中でより多くのトークンを消費し、モデル提供側が1トークンあたりの生産コストを下げる。AIプラットフォームの利益プールは、ハードウェア販売だけでなく、推論の運用そのものへ広がっている。

NVIDIAにとって、これは単純な逆風ではない。推論が儲かるほど、モデル企業はより多くの容量を確保したくなる。ラック、ネットワーク、メモリ、ソフトウェアを含む大規模推論基盤への支出は続く。NVIDIAの強さはGPU単体ではなく、供給制約の中で顧客が最短で大規模推論を立ち上げられるフルスタックにある。

一方で、70%超という数字は会社全体の利益率ではない。訓練費用、研究開発、人材コスト、将来のインフラ契約を含めれば、AIラボのキャッシュ消費はまだ軽くない。だからこの数字だけで、モデル企業が完成した高収益SaaSになったとは言えない。

それでも相場の焦点は変わる。以前は、誰がNVIDIAからGPUを確保できるかが中心だった。これからは、GPUの上で誰が高単価のワークロードを回し、どこまでマージンを守れるかに移っていく。NVIDIAを見るうえでも、GPU出荷だけでは足りない。顧客側のトークン経済がどれだけ厚くなり、その増分をNVIDIAがシステム価格とフルスタック支配でどこまで取り戻すかだ。

目前我认为未来三年每个月都会付费使用ChatGPT或者Claude Code，它已经进入我的日常我每天都会打开、每个月都会续费的东西，那我到底应该怎么衡量它的合理市值？多少钱算泡沫？多少钱算合理？多少钱又是值得大资金长期买入的锚点？
我想找到openai和Anthropic比较好的位置买入进去，当我还在持续高频使用它，我愿意持有股票；当我自己都不再使用它，或者它的不可替代性明显下降时，再考虑卖出。

当时我只觉得苹果手机系统好用，但我没有意识到，未来十几年我会一代又一代地更新苹果手机，也没有形成把这种消费习惯转化成投资逻辑的能力，当时也没钱没路子投资苹果股票。

经历过美股好公司长期上涨的逻辑以后，现在手里也有资金，不想错过 AI 这种解放生产力的大机会，我又害怕自己买在情绪最热估值最高的泡沫顶点。
这是目前我和大多数人都有的想法，我们应该如何去判断啊？

quoted_tweet.text:
@dejavucoder Entropy is H(p) = E_p[-log p(x)], your optimal expected code length when you know p. Shannon coding assigns symbol x a length of -log p(x), and on average you can't beat it.

Now suppose you don't know p. You believe it's q, so you build a code where x has length -log q(x). But the data is actually drawn from p, so your expected code length is:

H(p, q) = E_p[-log q(x)]
        = -sum over x of  p(x) * log q(x)
That's cross-entropy: the bits you actually pay using q's codebook on p's data.
KL is just the gap between what you pay and the optimum:
KL(p || q) = H(p, q) - H(p)
           = sum over x of  p(x) * log( p(x) / q(x) )

Or as a one-liner you could literally run:

kl = sum(p[x] * log(p[x] / q[x]) for x in support)

The story in three lines: H(p) is the floor, H(p,q) is what you actually pay with the wrong codebook, KL is the penalty — and it's always ≥ 0 because you can't beat the optimal code.

- Will Brown & Claude Opus 4.7
